message:
perf(quake): replace clip_polygon_near Vec with stack ClippedTriangle

The Quake E1M1 demo's per-triangle near-plane clipper used a
heap-allocated Vec<Vec3> for every clipped triangle:

  let mut out = Vec::with_capacity(poly.len() + 2);

That allocates inside the renderer's hot loop — once per visible
triangle per frame, with the sole consumer immediately discarding
the Vec after `draw_tri` / fan-triangulating into 3 calls.

A near-plane clip of a single triangle produces at most 4 vertices
(triangle in, polygon out: 0, 3, or 4 vertices depending on how
many vertices crossed the plane). So the allocation is gratuitous
— a fixed 4-vertex backing array suffices.

Replace `clip_polygon_near(&[Vec3], f32) -> Vec<Vec3>` with
`clip_triangle_near([Vec3; 3], f32) -> ClippedTriangle`:

- ClippedTriangle is a small POD: `verts: [Vec3; 4], len: usize`.
  Constructed once per call on the stack; no allocator traffic.
- `push(vertex)` debug-asserts the bound and silently no-ops on a
  full buffer (the algebra makes this impossible for a real
  triangle-vs-plane clip; debug_assert catches a regression that
  feeds a bad input).
- `as_slice()` and `len()` give the renderer the same read-only
  view it had over the Vec, with `for v in clipped.as_slice()`
  iteration unchanged.

The signature change from `&[Vec3]` to `[Vec3; 3]` makes the
"this only ever takes a triangle" precondition explicit at the
type level, so future maintainers can't accidentally feed it a
quad or pentagon (which would have silently overflowed the new
4-vertex backing buffer).

Caller (the visible-tri loop in `QuakeE1M1State::render`) updated
to pass the camera-space triangle as `[cam0, cam1, cam2]` and to
take the slice via `clipped.as_slice()` before the existing
`clipped.len() == 3` / fan-triangulation branch.

Three new tests pin the contract:
- fully visible triangle round-trips identically.
- one clipped vertex expands to a 4-vertex polygon, all on or
  past the near plane.
- fully hidden triangle yields an empty slice.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
